name: Zaynab
summary: A village maiden of Nayríz who, during the 1850 upheaval, disguised herself in male attire, cut her hair and took up a sword, fighting for some five months alongside the defenders of the Báb's cause; a celebrated heroine of the Nayríz episode.
importance_reason: A taught heroine of the Nayríz upheaval whose audacious story Shoghi Effendi recounts at length; appears in both DB and GPB.
characterizations: GPB: the resourcefulness and incredible audacity of Zaynab, a village maiden, who … disguised herself in male attire, cut off her locks, girt a sword about her waist … continued, during a period of five months, in the thick of the turmoil

name: Zaynu'l-'Ábidín Ḵhán, the Faḵhru'd-Dawlih
summary: A Qájár prince of the royal blood who, during Bahá'u'lláh's Baghdád period, was drawn into His circle of admirers and testified to the awe of His presence.
importance_reason: Named royal admirer of Bahá'u'lláh in Baghdád whose quoted testimony illustrates His magnetism, but only a minor recurring figure.
characterizations: GPB: "I know not how to explain it," another prince, Zaynu'l-'Ábidín Khán, the Fakhru'd-Dawlih … had affirmed, "were all the sorrows of the world to be crowded into my heart…"

name: Zaynu’l-‘Ábidín Khán
summary: The base and fanatical governor of Nayríz who, as Vaḥíd's foremost adversary, kindled and led the persecution of the Bábís in the first Nayríz upheaval of 1850.
importance_reason: Principal antagonist of the first Nayríz upheaval (28 mentions, rel_degree 9, both books); his cruelty drives a taught episode of Bábí history.
characterizations: GPB: the base and fanatical governor of Nayríz, Zaynu'l-'Ábidín Khán
GPB: Foremost among his [Vaḥíd's] perfidious adversaries, who kindled and fed the fire of this conflagration was the base and fanatical governor of Nayríz, Zaynu’l-‘Ábidín Ḵhán

name: Zayníl, son of Iskandar
summary: A Bábí agriculturist named among the defenders, mentioned once in The Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: Single-mention incidental named Bábí with no narrative role beyond his trade.

name: Zechariah the Prophet
summary: Post-exilic Hebrew prophet (6th century BC) whose prophecies of the "Branch" who would build the Temple of the Lord and the "Lord" who would be king over all the earth are interpreted by Shoghi Effendi as foretelling Bahá'u'lláh.
importance_reason: Biblical prophet cited in GPB as having foretold Bahá'u'lláh; his prophecies are taught as scriptural witness, though he is a peripheral figure to Bahá'í history itself.
characterizations: GPB: Zachariah as the "Branch" Who "shall grow up out of His [Bahá'u'lláh's] place," and "shall build the Temple of the Lord."
GPB: Zachariah had extolled Him [Bahá'u'lláh] as the "Lord" Who "shall be king over all the earth"

name: Zephaniah the Prophet
summary: Hebrew prophet of the reign of Josiah (7th century BC) whose description of the "day of Jehovah" — a day of wrath, trouble and thick darkness — Shoghi Effendi applies to the Day of Bahá'u'lláh.
importance_reason: Single-mention biblical prophet cited only for his "day of Jehovah" prophecy applied to Bahá'u'lláh; minor scriptural reference.
characterizations: GPB: to His [Bahá'u'lláh's] day Joel and Zephaniah had both referred as the "day of Jehovah," the latter describing it as "a day of wrath, a day of trouble and distress…"

name: Zoroaster
summary: Ancient prophet-founder of Zoroastrianism in Iran, recognized in the Bahá'í Faith as a Manifestation of God; Bahá'u'lláh is held to derive His descent in part from Zoroaster, who is said to have foretold the World-Savior Sháh-Bahrám.
importance_reason: A Manifestation of God in Bahá'í belief and a prophetic ancestor of Bahá'u'lláh whose prophecy of Sháh-Bahrám is taught; central to the principle of progressive revelation.
characterizations: GPB: of Zoroaster when awakened to His mission by a succession of seven visions
GPB: To Him [Bahá'u'lláh] Zoroaster must have alluded when … He foretold that a period of three thousand years of conflict and contention must needs precede the advent of the World-Savior Sháh-Bahrám

name: the Countess of Wemyss
summary: A British noblewoman mentioned once, with her sister, among those who received ‘Abdu'l-Bahá during His travels in the West.
importance_reason: Single-mention Western aristocratic acquaintance of ‘Abdu'l-Bahá with no further role.

name: the Ethiopian servant of the Báb
common_name: the Ethiopian servant of the Báb
summary: The devoted Ethiopian servant who attended the Báb, whose transformed manners and devotion impressed visitors; the Báb offered sacrifices in his name.
importance_reason: A memorable, recurring minor figure (6 mentions, both books) illustrating the Báb's household and influence, but unnamed and incidental.
characterizations: DB: I could not but marvel at the manners and the devoted attentions of that Ethiopian servant whose very life seemed to have been transformed by the regenerating influence of his Master.

name: the Governor of Fárs
summary: An early-20th-century governor of Fárs who sought to coerce the descendants of the Jahrum martyrs into declaring themselves Muslims, but desisted after their fearless message reached him.
importance_reason: Single-mention regional official in a brief post-1921 episode; title only, no lasting role.

name: the Grand Muftí of Egypt
summary: The Grand Muftí of Egypt who, around 1925 at the request of the Ministry of Justice, issued the landmark official fatvá declaring the Bahá'í community a distinct, non-Muslim religion — a pronouncement Shoghi Effendi cites as a milestone in the Faith's emancipation.
importance_reason: Author of the historically significant 1925 Egyptian fatvá recognizing the Faith as an independent religion — a taught landmark — though acting in his office rather than as a named protagonist.
characterizations: GPB: the official and no less remarkable fatvá (judgment) issued, at the request of the Ministry of Justice, by the Grand Muftí of Egypt
GPB: "We hereby declare that this Community is not to be regarded as Muslim, as shown by the beliefs which it professes…"

name: the Imám Ḥasan ibn ‘Alí
summary: The second Shí‘í Imám, grandson of the Prophet Muḥammad; invoked in The Dawn-Breakers only as the venerated ancestor from whom a believer's mother claimed lineal descent.
importance_reason: Major figure in Islam but appears in the corpus only incidentally as a claimed ancestor (2 mentions); peripheral to Bahá'í history proper.
characterizations: DB: my mother was always recognised by the inhabitants of this town as a lineal descendant of the Imám Ḥasan … honoured, nay venerated, by every one of you

name: the Maharaja of Jhalawar
summary: The ruling Maharaja of the Indian princely state of Jhalawar (Bhawani Singh, r. 1899–1929) who paid ‘Abdu'l-Bahá many visits and hosted an elaborate dinner and reception in His honor.
importance_reason: Single-mention Indian princely host of ‘Abdu'l-Bahá; courteous acquaintance, no further role.

name: the Maharaja of Rajputana
summary: An Indian maharaja of Rajputana named once in passing among those associated with ‘Abdu'l-Bahá.
importance_reason: Bare single-mention title with no narrative detail.

name: the Maid of Heaven
aliases: the embodiment of the remembrance of the name of My Lord
summary: The symbolic Maiden — personification of the Most Great Spirit — whose vision descended upon Bahá'u'lláh in the Síyáh-Chál of Ṭihrán (c. 1852–53), proclaiming His mission to all creation; a central symbol of the inception of His revelation.
importance_reason: The pivotal symbolic figure of Bahá'u'lláh's revelatory vision in the Síyáh-Chál — a foundational, much-taught image of the Faith's birth — though a symbolic/spiritual personage rather than a historical person.
characterizations: GPB: descended upon, and revealed itself, personated by a "Maiden," to the agonized soul of Bahá'u'lláh
GPB: I beheld a Maiden — the embodiment of the remembrance of the name of My Lord — suspended in the air before Me

name: the Maliku’t-Tujjár of Shíráz
summary: The "chief of the merchants" of Shíráz, referred to once in The Dawn-Breakers as having interceded on a believer's behalf.
importance_reason: Single-mention titled merchant-official with only an incidental intercession noted.

name: the Minister of War (deported to Káshán)
common_name: the Minister of War (deported to Káshán)
summary: A disgraced and deported Minister of War to whose house Ṭáhirih was transferred from Bahá'u'lláh's residence, mentioned once.
importance_reason: Single-mention official whose only role is providing a place of refuge for Ṭáhirih.

name: the Muftí of Haifa
summary: The Muslim Muftí of Haifa who was received with kindness by ‘Abdu'l-Bahá and who, in 1921, headed the Muslim notables and delivered a funeral oration at ‘Abdu'l-Bahá's interment.
importance_reason: Minor recurring local dignitary (3 mentions) notable mainly for participating in ‘Abdu'l-Bahá's funeral; title only.
characterizations: GPB: nine speakers, who represented the Muslim, the Jewish and Christian Faiths, and who included the Muftí of Haifa, delivered their several funeral orations

name: the Niẓámu’l-Mulk (son of Mírzá Áqá Khán-i-Núrí)
summary: A son of the Grand Vizier Mírzá Áqá Khán-i-Núrí, mentioned once questioning his father about Bahá'u'lláh.
importance_reason: Single-mention figure identified only by relation to his father; trivial narrative role.

name: the Ná'ibu'l-Íyálih
summary: A Qájár prince of the royal blood numbered, during Bahá'u'lláh's Baghdád period, among the personages drawn into His circle.
importance_reason: Single-mention royal admirer listed alongside other princes; no individual story.

name: the Sartíp of the Fawj-i-Kalbát
summary: A colonel (Sartíp) of the Kalbát regiment, named once in The Dawn-Breakers with no further detail.
importance_reason: Bare single-mention military title with no narrative content.

name: the Sayfu'd-Dawlih
summary: A Qájár prince of the royal blood listed among the personages drawn into Bahá'u'lláh's circle in Baghdád.
importance_reason: Single-mention royal admirer named only in a list of princes.

name: the Siyyid-i-Qumí
summary: A siyyid who treacherously deserted the fort of Shaykh Ṭabarsí and was later struck down, mentioned once.
importance_reason: Single-mention deserter at Ṭabarsí with a brief anecdote of betrayal.

name: the Sálár
summary: A military commander whose rebellion threw Mashhad into renewed confusion; mentioned once (and noted elsewhere as son of the Áṣifu'd-Dawlih).
importance_reason: Minor episode participant (the Salar rebellion) providing background turmoil; single mention.
characterizations: DB: The city of Mashhad … was plunged again into confusion and distress [by the rebellion that the Sálár had provoked].

name: the S̱hujá'u'd-Dawlih
summary: A Qájár prince so charmed by the simple reception room of Bahá'u'lláh in Baghdád that he wished to build a duplicate in his own home in Káẓimayn.
importance_reason: Minor royal admirer (2 mentions) with one memorable anecdote about Bahá'u'lláh's reception room.
characterizations: GPB: It was this same reception room which … had so charmed the Shujá'u'd-Dawlih that he had expressed to his fellow-princes his intention of building a duplicate of it in his home in Káẓimayn

name: the Varaqatu’l-Firdaws (sister of Mullá Ḥusayn)
summary: Sister of Mullá Ḥusayn, surnamed Varaqatu'l-Firdaws ("Leaf of Paradise"), who was closely associated in Karbilá with Ṭáhirih.
importance_reason: Notable for kinship to Mullá Ḥusayn and close association with Ṭáhirih, but a minor figure (2 mentions, DB only).
characterizations: DB: The sister of Mullá Ḥusayn was surnamed 'Varaqatu'l-Firdaws' and was intimately associated, while in Karbilá, with Ṭáhirih.

name: the Vazír-Niẓám
summary: An ambitious Persian statesman who, with others, sought in vain to secure Bahá'u'lláh's support for his own political designs.
importance_reason: Single-mention ambitious official listed among those who tried to enlist Bahá'u'lláh; no developed story.

name: the Virgin Mary, Mother of Jesus
summary: The mother of Jesus, whose purity and innocence the Bahá'í teachings uphold; ranked by ‘Abdu'l-Bahá among the immortal heroines (with Sarah, Ásíyih, Fáṭimih and Ṭáhirih) who outshone every woman of their age.
importance_reason: Revered figure cited in GPB among the supreme heroines of religious history; significant as a comparison-point for Bahá'í women, though peripheral to the Faith's own narrative.
characterizations: GPB: comparable in rank to those immortal heroines such as Sarah, Ásíyih, the Virgin Mary, Fáṭimih and Ṭáhirih, each of whom has outshone every member of her sex in previous Dispensations

name: the acting governor of Ámul
summary: The acting governor of Ámul who secured Bahá'u'lláh's release from captivity, having an opening made in the wall to lead Him to his own house.
importance_reason: Single-mention sympathetic official with one humane act protecting Bahá'u'lláh.

name: the ambitious Armenian (founder of the “Scientific Society”)
common_name: the ambitious Armenian (founder of the “Scientific Society”)
summary: An ambitious Armenian in 1920s Egypt who tried to supplant the nascent Bahá'í community with a rival "Scientific Society" of his own devising, but failed utterly.
importance_reason: Single-mention minor opponent of the early Egyptian community whose scheme came to nothing.

name: the brother of Siyyid Murtaḍá (martyr of Ṭabarsí)
summary: A martyr of Shaykh Ṭabarsí who fought beside Mullá Ḥusayn; remembered through his brother Siyyid Murtaḍá's invocation.
importance_reason: Single-mention Ṭabarsí martyr identified only by relation to his brother.

name: the governor of Yazd (1266 A.H.)
summary: The young and inexperienced governor of Yazd (c. 1850) whose support was enlisted in a local power struggle, mentioned in connection with Vaḥíd's Yazd episode.
importance_reason: Minor episode participant (5 mentions) in the Yazd disturbances; an unnamed official with a supporting role.

name: the headman of Kawmu’ṣ-Ṣa‘áyidih
summary: The village headman of Kawmu'ṣ-Ṣa‘áyidih in Egypt whose religious fanaticism, kindled by the formation of a Bahá'í assembly, led to accusations and mob violence against the local believers.
importance_reason: Single-mention local instigator in an Egyptian persecution episode; title only.

name: the leading mullá of Ámul
summary: The chief cleric of Ámul who bitterly denounced Bahá'u'lláh in the local Mázandarání dialect during His captivity there.
importance_reason: Minor opponent (2 mentions) with a brief denunciation scene; unnamed.

name: the notary of Kawmu’ṣ-Ṣa‘áyidih
summary: The village notary of Kawmu'ṣ-Ṣa‘áyidih who, as authorized religious plaintiff, brought the suit demanding that three Bahá'í men's Muslim wives be divorced from them — the case leading to the Beba court ruling.
importance_reason: Single-mention initiator of the Egyptian divorce suit that produced a landmark ruling; an instrumental but unnamed minor figure.

name: the sifter of wheat of Iṣfahán
common_name: the sifter of wheat of Iṣfahán
summary: The first person in Iṣfahán to embrace the Cause of the Báb — a humble sifter of wheat who unreservedly accepted the Message the moment he heard it; a celebrated example of the receptivity of the lowly.
importance_reason: A single-mention figure but a famous, oft-cited symbol of pure-hearted recognition (and the namesake of this project); memorable beyond his brief appearance.
characterizations: DB: The first to embrace the Cause of the Báb in that city was a man, a sifter of wheat, who, as soon as the Call reached his ears, unreservedly accepted the Message.

name: the sister of the Minister of War (hostess to Ṭáhirih)
common_name: the sister of the Minister of War (hostess to Ṭáhirih)
summary: A friend of Bahá'u'lláh's household, sister of the Minister of War, who was asked to act as hostess to Ṭáhirih; mentioned once.
importance_reason: Single-mention supporter whose only role is sheltering Ṭáhirih.

name: the siyyid of Káshán (Bábí informer)
summary: A treacherous siyyid of Káshán who betrayed the names and addresses of some fifty believers to the ‘ulamá, mentioned in connection with Siyyid Muḥammad.
importance_reason: Minor antagonist (2 mentions) with one act of betrayal; unnamed informer.

name: the siyyid of Yazd (martyr of Bárfurús̱h)
common_name: the siyyid of Yazd (martyr of Bárfurús̱h)
summary: A staunch siyyid supporter from Yazd, killed by enemy fire near Bárfurúsh, who had walked all the way from Mashhad.
importance_reason: Single-mention Ṭabarsí-era martyr identified only by origin; likely the same figure as the next entry.
characterizations: DB: a bullet … pierced the breast of one of his companions, a siyyid from Yazd who had walked all the way from Mashhad to that place

name: the siyyid who walked on foot from Ḵhurásán to Bárfurús̱h
common_name: the siyyid who walked on foot from Ḵhurásán to Bárfurús̱h
summary: A siyyid who walked on foot all the way from Khurásán to Bárfurúsh, where he fell to an enemy bullet; a martyr of the Ṭabarsí campaign.
importance_reason: Single-mention pedestrian martyr; very likely identical to the 'siyyid of Yazd (martyr of Bárfurús̱h)' above.

name: the widow of Siyyid Káẓim-i-Rashtí
summary: The revered widow of Siyyid Káẓim-i-Rashtí, born in Shíráz, who was the first woman of Karbilá to recognize the Cause and who died three days afterward, as her husband had.
importance_reason: Minor but notable single-mention figure — first woman of Karbilá to recognize the Faith, won over by Ṭáhirih.

name: the young man from Bahnimír
summary: A young man from Bahnimír slain in Bárfurúsh two days after Quddús abandoned the fort of Shaykh Ṭabarsí.
importance_reason: Single-mention martyr identified only by his town.

name: the Áṣifu’d-Dawlih
summary: Elder maternal uncle of the Sháh and governor of the province, father of the rebel Sálár; mentioned once. (Almost certainly the same individual as 'Áṣifu'd-Dawlih, governor of Ḵhurásán' below.)
importance_reason: Single-mention provincial governor and royal kinsman; background figure. Likely duplicate of the Khurásán entry.
characterizations: DB: the Sálár, son of the Áṣifu’d-Dawlih, the elder maternal uncle of the Sháh and governor of the province

name: the Ṣadr-i-Ardibílí
summary: A presumptuous and arrogant ecclesiastical leader of Persia whose treacherous conduct drew Muḥammad Sháh's disapproval, who banished him to obscurity in Qum.
importance_reason: Minor clerical figure (3 mentions) noted for arrogance and royal censure; supporting antagonist.
characterizations: DB: a man notoriously presumptuous and one of the most arrogant among the ecclesiastical leaders of Persia

name: the Ṣáḥib-Díván
summary: A high official in whose presence Mullá ‘Abdu'lláh's trial took place in Ṭihrán; mentioned three times.
importance_reason: Minor recurring official (3 mentions) serving as a trial authority; no developed individual story.

name: Áqá Buzurg-i-Nís̱hápúrí
summary: The seventeen-year-old who bore Bahá'u'lláh's Tablet (the Lawḥ-i-Sulṭán) to Náṣiri'd-Dín Sháh and was tortured to death (1869); surnamed Badí' ("the Wonderful") and the "Pride of Martyrs," he is reckoned among the Apostles of Bahá'u'lláh.
importance_reason: One of the most celebrated heroes and martyrs of the Bahá'í dispensation — bearer of the Tablet to the Shah, an Apostle of Bahá'u'lláh whose sacrifice Bahá'u'lláh called 'the salt of My Tablets.'
characterizations: GPB: Áqá Buzurg of Khurásán, the illustrious 'Badí‘' (Wonderful) … surnamed the 'Pride of Martyrs'; the seventeen-year-old bearer of the Tablet addressed to Náṣiri'd-Dín Sháh
Bahaipedia: Badí' is most famous for being the bearer of a tablet written by Baháʼu'lláh to Nasiri'd-Din Shah, for which he was tortured and killed at the age of 17.

name: Áqá Dín-Muḥammad
summary: A Bábí martyr upon whose head boiling oil was poured, yet who remained silent under torture; mentioned once.
importance_reason: Single-mention martyr with one brief torture anecdote.

name: Áqá Ján (Turkish artillery accomplice)
summary: A follower turned Azalí accomplice who, with Siyyid Muḥammad, wrote anonymous letters perverting Bahá'u'lláh's writings and falsely accusing Him of conspiring with foreign powers to seize Constantinople.
importance_reason: Minor antagonist (4 mentions) in the Adrianople-era calumnies against Bahá'u'lláh; an accomplice rather than principal.
characterizations: GPB: Anonymous letters (written by this same Siyyid and by an accomplice, Áqá Ján, serving in the Turkish artillery) which perverted the writings of Bahá'u'lláh

name: Áqá Ján Big-i-Khamsih
summary: Colonel of the body-guard who volunteered to replace Sám Khán and command the regiment that carried out the Báb's execution in 1850; he lost his life six years later during the British bombardment of Muḥammarih.
importance_reason: Notable secondary figure of the Báb's martyrdom — the officer who fired the second, fatal volley — with a recorded retributive death (5 mentions, both books).
characterizations: GPB: Áqá Ján-i-Khamsih, colonel of the body-guard, volunteered to replace him [Sám Khán]
GPB: The head of that regiment, Áqá Ján Big, lost his life, six years after the Báb's martyrdom, during the bombardment of Muḥammarih by the British naval forces.

name: Áqá Ján, son of ‘Alí-Aṣg̱har Ḵhán
summary: A man stoned and beaten to death amid the Nayríz persecutions; son of ‘Alí-Aṣghar Khán and nephew of the governor Zaynu'l-‘Ábidín Khán. Mentioned once.
importance_reason: Single-mention victim identified by family relation; incidental.

name: Áqá Khán (son of ‘Alí-Aṣg̱har Khán)
summary: Son of ‘Alí-Aṣghar Khán (elder brother of the Nayríz governor), who suffered the same fate as his father; mentioned once.
importance_reason: Single-mention figure named only by family relation in the Nayríz context.

name: Áqá Khán-i-Ismá‘ílí
summary: Leader of an Ismá‘ílí revolt in Kirmán (historically the Aga Khan I), referenced once as background political turmoil.
importance_reason: Single-mention figure cited only for his Kirmán revolt as contextual unrest; not a Bábí/Bahá'í actor.

name: Áqá Mírzá Muḥammad-Hádíy-i-Dawlatábádí
summary: A notorious Azalí (1846–1908) whom Mírzá Yaḥyá appointed as his successor, but who, terrified by the martyrdom of Mírzá Ashraf, publicly repudiated the Bábí Faith and Mírzá Yaḥyá himself from the pulpit for four days.
importance_reason: Notable Azalí figure illustrating the collapse of Mírzá Yaḥyá's succession, with a memorable public-recantation anecdote (2 mentions, GPB).
characterizations: GPB: the perfidious Mírzá Hádíy-i-Dawlat-Ábádí, a notorious Azalí, who … during four consecutive days … proclaimed from the pulpit-top … his complete repudiation of the Bábí Faith, as well as of Mírzá Yaḥyá, his benefactor

name: Áqá Mírzá Muḥammad-‘Alí, son of Siyyid Aḥmad
common_name: Mírzá Muḥammad-‘Alí, son of Siyyid Aḥmad
aliases: Áqá Mírzá Muḥammad-‘Alí, son of Siyyid Aḥmad
summary: A young Bábí, much loved by Quddús, whose head was carried off by a cannonball as he stood at the entrance of the fort of Shaykh Ṭabarsí.
importance_reason: Single-mention Ṭabarsí martyr with a vivid death scene; affection of Quddús noted (2 mentions).
characterizations: DB: whose head was blown off by the ball from a cannon as he was standing at the entrance of the fort, and who, because of his tender age, was greatly loved and admired by Quddús

name: Áqá Riḍáy-i-Qannád-i-Shírází
summary: One of Bahá'u'lláh's staunchest companions, exiled with Him from Baghdád to ‘Akká, who served as a recorder/chronicler of pivotal scenes such as the declaration in the Garden of Riḍván.
importance_reason: A faithful companion and eyewitness chronicler whose quoted accounts of Bahá'u'lláh's exile are taught source material (2 mentions, GPB).
characterizations: GPB: writes Áqá Riḍá, one of the stoutest supporters of Bahá'u'lláh, exiled with him all the way from Baghdád to ‘Akká

name: Áqá Siyyid Abú-Ṭálib
summary: A wealthy Bábí of the Nayríz region who was chained, exiled to Ma‘dan by the governor, and there poisoned by Ḥájí Mírzá Náṣir; mentioned once.
importance_reason: Single-mention Nayríz-area martyr with a brief account of his end.

name: Áqá Siyyid Muḥammad-i-Qazvíní, the Mujtahid
aliases: Áqá Siyyid Muḥammad, the Mujtahid (of Qazvín)
summary: A great mujtahid of Qazvín who confirmed and conferred the rank of mujtahid on Ḥájí Mullá Ṣáliḥ-i-Baraqání; mentioned once.
importance_reason: Single-mention clerical figure providing background on the Baraqání family's standing.

name: Áqá Siyyid Qásim
summary: Father of Siyyid Káẓim-i-Rashtí; a merchant of repute, mentioned once in a genealogical note.
importance_reason: Single-mention figure named only as the father of Siyyid Káẓim.

name: Áqá Siyyid Ḥusayn, ‘the beloved’
summary: A ten-year-old child slain before his father's eyes at the fort of Shaykh Ṭabarsí; remembered as 'the beloved.'
importance_reason: Single-mention child martyr whose poignant death is one of the memorable images of Ṭabarsí.

name: Áqá S̱hayḵh Hádí (uncle of the wife of Vaḥíd)
common_name: Áqá S̱hayḵh Hádí (uncle of the wife of Vaḥíd)
summary: Uncle of Vaḥíd's wife, named once in The Dawn-Breakers with no further detail.
importance_reason: Bare single-mention figure identified only by family relation.

name: Áqá Ḥusayn, son of the deceased chief
common_name: Áqá Ḥusayn, son of the deceased chief
summary: A seven-year-old boy, son of a slain Bábí chief, brought before the Sardár; mentioned once.
importance_reason: Single-mention child figure in a brief Ṭabarsí-era scene.

name: Áqá ‘Alí
summary: A Bahá'í of Yazd executed (d. 1903) before a frenzied crowd during the persecutions there.
importance_reason: Single-mention martyr of the 1903 Yazd upheaval; brief execution scene.

name: Áqá ‘Azíz
summary: A Bábí whose house, one of the strongholds of the city, was broken into by the fourth regiment and burned to the ground; mentioned once.
importance_reason: Single-mention figure noted only for the destruction of his house.

name: Ármin Vámbéry
summary: Famous Hungarian orientalist, linguist and traveler (1832–1913) who met ‘Abdu'l-Bahá in Budapest in 1913 and, in a letter of warm admiration, declared his allegiance to the Faith — regarded as the first Hungarian Bahá'í; cited by Shoghi Effendi as evidence of the Faith's progress.
importance_reason: A distinguished Western scholar whose public tribute to ‘Abdu'l-Bahá and embrace of the Faith are cited as a sign of its spread; notable beyond his thin corpus presence (2 mentions, GPB).
characterizations: GPB: met on a number of occasions the famous Orientalist Prof. Arminius Vambery
Wikipedia/Bahaipedia: regarded as the first Hungarian Bahá'í, having declared his allegiance in a letter to ‘Abdu'l-Bahá in the last weeks of his life

name: Ásíyih Khánum
summary: The wife of Bahá'u'lláh (1820–1886), titled Navváb and the Most Exalted Leaf (Varaqiy-i-‘Ulyá); mother of ‘Abdu'l-Bahá, the Purest Branch (Mírzá Mihdí) and Bahíyyih Khánum, revered as the paragon of devotion whose afflictions ‘Abdu'l-Bahá likened to Isaiah's 54th chapter.
importance_reason: Wife of Bahá'u'lláh and matriarch of the Holy Family — mother of ‘Abdu'l-Bahá — a foremost figure of Bahá'í history despite a modest corpus count (5 mentions).
characterizations: GPB: the saintly Navváb, she to whose dire afflictions, as attested by ‘Abdu'l-Bahá in a Tablet, the 54th chapter of the Book of Isaiah has, in its entirety, borne witness
Bahaipedia: viewed by Bahá'ís as the paragon of a devoted mother and wife; called, with Bahá'u'lláh, the 'Mother of Consolation.'

name: Ásíyih bint Muzáḥim (wife of Pharaoh)
summary: The believing wife of Pharaoh, honored in Islamic tradition; ranked by ‘Abdu'l-Bahá among the immortal heroines (with Sarah, the Virgin Mary, Fáṭimih and Ṭáhirih) who outshone every woman of their Dispensation.
importance_reason: Single-mention figure of antiquity cited only as a comparison-point among supreme religious heroines.

name: Áḵhúnd Mullá Báqir
aliases: Áḵhúnd Mullá Báqir, Pish-namáz of the district
summary: An ákhúnd serving as Pish-namáz (prayer-leader) of the district, named once in The Dawn-Breakers. (Distinct from the Letter of the Living Mullá Báqir-i-Tabrízí.)
importance_reason: Single-mention local cleric with no developed role.

name: Áḵhúnd Mullá Muḥammad-Ṣádiq-i-Ḵhurásání
summary: A Bábí of Khurásán who was bound in chains and taken captive; mentioned once. (Likely the figure also known as Muqaddas-i-Khurásání / Ismu'lláhu'l-Aṣdaq — flagged, not merged.)
importance_reason: Single-mention captive Bábí; may correspond to a more prominent figure under another name (Ismu'lláhu'l-Aṣdaq).
characterizations: DB: They bound in chains … Áḵhúnd Mullá Muḥammad-Ṣádiq-i-Ḵhurásání.

name: Áḵhúnd Mullá ‘Alí-i-Qazvíní
summary: A cleric of Qazvín quoted assessing another's character ('an ignorant man with a pure heart'); mentioned once.
importance_reason: Single-mention cleric providing a quoted aside; minor.

name: Áṣifu’d-Dawlih (governor of Ḵhurásán)
summary: Governor of Khurásán and brother of the King's mother who, feeling his security threatened, came to court to protest at the King's feet; mentioned once. (Almost certainly the same person as 'the Áṣifu'd-Dawlih' above.)
importance_reason: Single-mention royal-kinsman governor; very likely a duplicate of 'the Áṣifu'd-Dawlih' entry above.
characterizations: DB: the governor of Ḵhurásán, Áṣifu’d-Dawlih, brother of the King's mother, feeling that his security was threatened … arrived at the Court humbly to protest at the feet of the King.

name: Íldirím Mírzá
summary: A treacherous figure in Luristán who brought about the sudden end of a siyyid's career of service; mentioned several times.
importance_reason: Minor antagonist (4 mentions, DB) tied to one episode of treachery in Luristán.

name: Ímán-Qulí
summary: A Bábí companion savagely mutilated by the enemy after being betrayed by his own brother; mentioned once.
importance_reason: Single-mention martyr with a poignant betrayal-and-mutilation anecdote.

name: Íṭábakí-C̱húpán (martyr of Savád-Kúh)
summary: A martyr associated with Savád-Kúh, named (with his son) twice in The Dawn-Breakers.
importance_reason: Bare double-mention martyr with no narrative beyond his name and son.

name: Ḏhu’l-Faqár Ḵhán
summary: Owner of a fort near Sháhrúd where a believer was to be sheltered until Bahá'u'lláh returned to the capital; mentioned once. (Possibly the same as 'Ḏhu'l-Faqár Ḵhán-i-Karávulí' below.)
importance_reason: Single-mention figure named for his fort; possible duplicate of the Karávulí entry.
characterizations: DB: take him with me to the fort of Ḏhu’l-Faqár Ḵhán, situated in the vicinity of Sháhrúd

name: Ḏhu’l-Faqár Ḵhán-i-Karávulí
summary: A figure named once in The Dawn-Breakers with the nisba Karávulí and no further detail. (Possibly identical to 'Ḏhu'l-Faqár Ḵhán' above.)
importance_reason: Bare single-mention name; possible duplicate of the preceding Ḏhu'l-Faqár Ḵhán entry.

name: Ḥabíbu’lláh Mírzá
summary: A prince of the royal blood and convert who consecrated his life to the Faith and was martyred at Dawlat-Ábád — slain with a hatchet and his corpse set on fire — during ‘Abdu'l-Bahá's ministry.
importance_reason: Single-mention royal-blood martyr with a vivid death; notable as a princely convert but thin in the corpus.
characterizations: GPB: a prince of the royal blood, Ḥabíbu’lláh Mírzá by name, a convert to the Faith who had consecrated his life to its service, was slain with a hatchet and his corpse set on fire

name: Ḥabíbu’lláh Ḵhán-i-Afg̱hán
summary: An officer under ‘Abdu'lláh Khán-i-Turkamán who perished alongside him; mentioned twice.
importance_reason: Double-mention subordinate officer named only in a casualty list.

name: Ḥasan (bearer of the body of Mullá Ḥusayn)
common_name: Ḥasan (bearer of the body of Mullá Ḥusayn)
summary: A Ṭabarsí companion who, with Qulí and Iskandar of Zanján, bore Mullá Ḥusayn's body into the fort and laid it at the feet of Quddús; later led by a halter through the streets of Mashhad on the chief constable's orders.
importance_reason: Minor but recurring Ṭabarsí figure (4 mentions) tied to the memorable burial of Mullá Ḥusayn and a public humiliation.
characterizations: DB: Ḥasan and Qulí, who, together with a man named Iskandar, a native of Zanján, bore the body of Mullá Ḥusayn to the fort on the night of his martyrdom and placed it at the feet of Quddús.

name: Ḥasan (servant of Vaḥíd)
summary: The personal servant of Vaḥíd who saddled his horse and was entrusted with conveying belongings outside the city gate; mentioned several times. (Distinct from Ḥasan, bearer of Mullá Ḥusayn's body — flagged.)
importance_reason: Minor recurring servant (4 mentions) in Vaḥíd's Nayríz narrative; a distinct Ḥasan from the Ṭabarsí bearer.

name: Ḥasan Effendi
summary: A Turkish captain who, with his soldiers, escorted Bahá'u'lláh and His family on the four-day journey to Gallipoli; on taking his leave he received Bahá'u'lláh's pronouncement that the territory 'will pass out of [the king's] hands.'
importance_reason: Minor official (2 mentions) memorable as the recipient of Bahá'u'lláh's prophetic message to the Sultan en route to ‘Akká.
characterizations: GPB: Bahá'u'lláh made the following pronouncement to Ḥasan Effendi … 'Tell the king that this territory will pass out of his hands'
